name: E — Object-Capability Language
summary: Mark Miller's E (1997+) is the canonical object-capability language and the source of most modern ocap vocabulary: sealer/unsealer rights amplification, the Granovetter operator, eventual sends, vats, and CapTP. Historical/reference status; its ideas shipped to production via Cap'n Proto, Spritely Goblins, Agoric, SES/Hardened JS, and WASI components.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
**E** (Mark S. Miller et al., 1997+) is the canonical object-capability programming language and the source from which most of the modern ocap vocabulary descends. E formalises the thesis that authority is conveyed **only by holding object references**: there is no global namespace, no `File.open`, no `process.getenv` — every effect requires receiving a reference from a creator.

The core security argument E established: holding an object reference *is* the permission to invoke methods on that object. Therefore the reference graph (which references which objects can name) statically bounds authority, and authority can only grow via the **Granovetter operator** — introduction through an existing reference. This is formalised as a lambda calculus security kernel and is the conceptual foundation cited by every later ocap design.

sections.title: Primitives — sealer/unsealer pairs, eventual send, vats
sections.content:
E introduced the language-level primitives that have become standard ocap vocabulary:

- **Sealer/unsealer pairs** — a rights-amplification primitive that lets two parties hold capabilities whose joint use exceeds either alone. The sealer can put a value in a box; only the matching unsealer can open it. This is the basis for E's mint/purse currency pattern (the mint sealer can create money; the unsealer holds the deposit method) and for the broader pattern of "capability brand-checking without losing unforgeability."
- **Eventual send (`<-`)** — non-blocking message send returning a promise; the foundation of *promise pipelining*, which E pioneered and Cap'n Proto / CapTP standardised on the wire.
- **Vats** — units of single-threaded sequential execution that own a set of objects; cross-vat references are remote and may only be invoked via eventual send. The vat is E's actor-equivalent and the addressing unit for distributed ocap.
- **The Granovetter operator** — "only `introduce(a, b)` via an existing reference can grow the reference graph." Formalised as a lambda calculus security kernel and used to prove confinement bounds.

sections.title: Lineage and status
sections.content:
Status (as of 2026-04): E itself is largely historical/reference — the canonical Java-implemented runtime is no longer actively developed. Its **influence is everywhere** in the modern ocap stack:

- **Cap'n Proto / CapTP** is E's wire protocol generalised to a typed binary IDL.
- **SES / Hardened JS** is the E discipline retrofitted as a JavaScript subset (`Compartment`, `lockdown()`).
- **Spritely Goblins** is a direct port of E's vat/eventual-send/CapTP model to Racket and Guile Scheme.
- **Monte** is a Python-syntax language with E semantics.
- **Agoric** is a smart-contract platform built on hardened-JS with E's security model.
- **WASI Component Model** independently rediscovers E's no-ambient-authority discipline at the binary level (resource handles play the role of object references).

Mark Miller's continued role at Agoric and on TC39 SES makes him the through-line: the same person who designed E in the 1990s now drives Hardened JS standardisation. The reference work for ocap design.
